7.note: Sarah was barren; she had no children — the barren wife whose son becomes the covenant instrument is the governing type of Luke 1. Elizabeth recapitulates Sarah (barrenness, old age, divine opening of the womb) as the mother of the covenant forerunner, just as Sarah was the mother of the covenant heir.

The LORD had closed Hannah's womb — Hannah is the closer parallel: like Elizabeth, she was the barren wife of a faithful Levitical husband, received a specific prophetic promise, and her miraculous son (Samuel) was set apart for God's service as a forerunner of the Davidic era. The Magnificat (vv.46-55) will explicitly model itself on Hannah's prayer.

13.note: An angel appeared and said: You are barren and childless, but you are going to conceive and give birth to a son — the angel's announcement to Manoah's wife (Samson's mother) follows the same sequence as the announcement to Zechariah: barrenness stated, conception promised, son's name given, son's role declared, Nazarite consecration required. Luke presents John's annunciation as the fulfilment of this type.

15.note: He is never to take wine or other fermented drink — the Nazarite vow (Num 6:1-21) required abstention from all grape products as the outward mark of total consecration to YHWH. John is consecrated from the womb, as Samson was (Judg 13:4-5) and possibly Samuel (1 Sam 1:11 LXX). The vow marks him as wholly set apart, outside the normal channels of priestly or social life.

Now see to it that you drink no wine or other fermented drink... because the boy is to be a Nazirite — the command to Samson's mother directly parallels the angel's word about John; both sons are Nazarites from the womb, consecrated to a particular divine mission before birth.

17.note: He will go before him in the spirit and power of Elijah, to turn the hearts of fathers to their children — the Malachi Elijah-promise fulfilled in John the Baptist's preparatory ministry

I will send my messenger, who will prepare the way before me — the companion text to Mal 4:5-6; the messenger who prepares the way of the Lord is identified with the Elijah figure. John's mission (prepare the way) fuses both Malachi texts.
Elijah before the great day of the Lord — John the Baptist

19.note: I heard a human voice calling across the Ulai: Gabriel, tell this man the meaning of the vision — Gabriel is named in only two OT contexts: Dan 8:16 and Dan 9:21. Both are interpretive angelic appearances to Daniel in response to prayer, delivering eschatological revelation. Gabriel's appearance to Zechariah ('I stand in the presence of God') signals that this is an event of similar eschatological weight.

Gabriel... came to me in swift flight about the time of the evening sacrifice — Daniel 9 is the closest structural parallel: Gabriel arrives at the time of the evening offering (exactly Zechariah's situation in the temple), responds to Daniel's prayer, and announces a seventy-weeks timetable for the coming of the anointed one. Luke's annunciation scene echoes this in setting, character, and eschatological content.

25.note: God has taken away my disgrace — Rachel's identical declaration when Joseph was born, after years of barrenness and shame. Elizabeth's words are a conscious echo of Rachel's; both women experienced barrenness as public disgrace in an honor-shame culture where fertility was the primary measure of a wife's value, and both interpret the miraculous birth as divine removal of that shame.

32.note: I will raise up your offspring to succeed you... and I will establish his throne forever — Nathan's oracle to David (the foundational Davidic covenant promise) is fulfilled in the annunciation: 'The Lord God will give him the throne of his father David, and he will reign over Jacob's descendants forever.' The eternal Davidic throne promised in 2 Sam 7 is explicitly applied to Jesus.

Of the greatness of his government and peace there will be no end. He will reign on David's throne... forever — Isaiah's throne-promise (already cited by Matthew for Galilee) underpins the annunciation's royal language. 'Son of the Most High' and the eternal throne together invoke Isaiah's royal-Davidic prophecy.
Scepter from Judah forever — throne of David given to Jesus
David's son on his throne forever — throne given to Jesus

33.note: His dominion is an everlasting dominion that will not pass away, and his kingdom is one that will never be destroyed — Daniel's vision of the Son of Man receiving eternal kingship from the Ancient of Days provides the language of 'kingdom that will never end.' Luke's annunciation language fuses the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7) with the Daniel 7 vision of eternal dominion.

35.note: Moses could not enter the tent of meeting because the cloud had settled on it, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle — the Greek episkiazō (overshadow) in Luke 1:35 is the same word the LXX uses in Exod 40:35 for the Shekinah cloud covering the tabernacle. Mary as the new tabernacle — the dwelling place of the divine presence — is the typological point: as the glory filled the tent, the Spirit comes upon Mary.

The Spirit of God was hovering over the waters — the Spirit's creative activity at the first creation (ruach hovering over the formless void) is echoed in the Spirit 'coming upon' Mary for the new creation. The annunciation is a creation event: the Spirit who initiated the first creation initiates the new creation through the virginal conception.

37.note: Is anything too hard for the LORD? — YHWH's response to Sarah's laughter about conceiving in old age is the verbal precedent for Gabriel's assurance to Mary: 'no word from God will ever fail.' The annunciation to Mary echoes the annunciation to Sarah; both women receive impossible birth promises, both receive divine assurance of God's power over biological impossibility.

42.note: Most blessed of women is Jael, the wife of Heber the Kenite, most blessed of tent-dwelling women — Deborah's victory song declares Jael 'blessed among women' for delivering Israel from its enemy. Elizabeth's identical declaration over Mary situates Mary in the line of women through whom YHWH accomplished decisive salvific acts, but now for an even greater deliverance.

You are blessed by the Most High God above all women on earth — Judith is blessed 'above all women' after delivering Israel by killing Holofernes. The formula 'blessed among/above women' was attached to women who brought Israel salvation through divine enablement; Elizabeth applies it to the one carrying the ultimate salvation.

46.note: The Magnificat draws throughout from Hannah's song — Magnifies/exalts; proud scattered; mighty pulled down; humble exalted; hungry filled; rich sent away empty — the Hannah pattern applied to Mary's eschatological joy
His mercy is from generation to generation — YHWH's steadfast love from everlasting to everlasting for those who fear him; Mary's blessing echoes the Psalmic generational covenant-faithfulness

My heart rejoices in the LORD; in the LORD my horn is lifted high — Hannah's prayer (1 Sam 2:1-10) is the structural model for the Magnificat. Both songs: open with the speaker's soul/heart exulting in God; move through divine reversals (low exalted, proud scattered, hungry filled, powerful brought down); ground the praise in God's covenant faithfulness; conclude with the Davidic promise. Mary's song is a typological realization of Hannah's.
Mary's Magnificat echoes Hannah's prayer of praise

47.note: I will rejoice in the LORD, I will be joyful in God my Savior — Habakkuk's closing song of trust amid catastrophe uses identical language: 'my spirit rejoices in God my Savior.' Both are songs of joy grounded not in present circumstances but in divine character and covenant faithfulness.

49.note: He provided redemption for his people; he ordained his covenant forever — holy and awesome is his name — the Psalm's declaration that YHWH's name is holy and awesome in the context of his covenant provision is echoed in Mary's 'holy is his name.' The Magnificat draws on the hymnic tradition of Ps 111 for its doxological affirmations.

50.note: But from everlasting to everlasting the LORD's love is with those who fear him — the Psalm's statement that covenant mercy extends across generations to those who fear YHWH is the direct antecedent for 'his mercy extends to those who fear him, from generation to generation.' Mary's Magnificat integrates this psalmic covenant-mercy tradition.

51.note: He has scattered the proud in the thoughts of their hearts — YHWH scatters his enemies in power (Ps 89:10); the Magnificat's reversal-theology draws from the royal covenant Psalms

52.note: He has brought down the mighty from their thrones — YHWH strips kings of their authority (Job 12:19); the reversal-theology of the Magnificat synthesizes wisdom and prophetic traditions of divine overturning

The LORD sends poverty and wealth; he humbles and he exalts. He raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap — Hannah's prayer articulates the same divine reversal that structures Mary's Magnificat: rulers brought down, humble lifted up. The Magnificat's social reversals are drawn directly from Hannah's vocabulary of YHWH's overturning of human power structures.

53.note: Those who were full hire themselves out for food, but those who were hungry are hungry no more — Hannah's prayer provides the specific filled/hungry reversal that Mary echoes. The Magnificat's 'filled the hungry with good things but sent the rich away empty' is a near-quotation of Hannah's reversal-language, transposed from past tense (Hannah describing what God did) to aorist (Mary describing what God is doing).

54.note: He has helped his servant Israel in remembrance of his mercy — Israel as YHWH's servant (Isa 41:8); the Magnificat reads the incarnation as the fulfillment of YHWH's servant-help promise

But you, Israel, my servant, Jacob, whom I have chosen, you descendants of Abraham my friend — I took you from the ends of the earth... I said, You are my servant — Isaiah's servant-Israel language (the covenant language of election and sustained help) underlies 'he has helped his servant Israel.' The Magnificat situates the annunciation within Second Exodus theology: Israel as YHWH's servant being helped, now in its ultimate mode.

55.note: You will be faithful to Jacob, and show love to Abraham, as you pledged on oath to our ancestors in days long ago — Micah's closing covenantal declaration (fidelity to Abraham and Jacob) is echoed in 'just as he promised our ancestors.' The Magnificat closes by grounding the annunciation in the Abrahamic covenant — the oldest layer of the divine promise that the entire narrative is fulfilling.

59.note: Every male among you who is eight days old must be circumcised — the Abrahamic covenant's sign was circumcision on the eighth day (Gen 17:12; Lev 12:3). John's circumcision on the eighth day places his entry into the covenant community within the foundational Abrahamic rite. The naming occasion (culturally associated with circumcision) becomes the moment when the prophetic name overrides the family tradition.

68.note: Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, for he has visited and redeemed his people — the Benedictus of Zechariah; the Psalmic blessing of YHWH for his redemptive acts now applied to the birth of John and Jesus

69.note: Here I will make a horn grow for David and set up a lamp for my anointed one — the 'horn of salvation in the house of his servant David' in the Benedictus draws on Ps 132:17, the covenantal psalm promising that YHWH will raise up a Davidic ruler from Zion. The horn (keren) is the standard OT symbol of military strength and royal power.

He will give strength to his king and exalt the horn of his anointed — Hannah's prayer closes with the first occurrence of 'his anointed' (mashiach) in the OT, in the context of a horn being raised for the king. The Benedictus's 'horn of salvation' citation closes the loop: Hannah's prayer (which opened the messianic-king hope) is now being fulfilled in the forerunner's birth announcement.
David's line established forever — horn of salvation from David's house

72.note: I will establish my covenant as an everlasting covenant between me and you and your descendants after you — the Abrahamic covenant as an 'everlasting covenant' is what Zechariah identifies as the theological ground for the coming salvation: 'to remember his holy covenant.' The entire redemptive movement is a covenant-memory event — YHWH fulfilling what he swore to Abraham.

73.note: I swear by myself, declares the LORD, that because you have done this and have not withheld your son... I will surely bless you — the oath YHWH swore to Abraham after the binding of Isaac is the specific oath Zechariah identifies: 'the oath he swore to our father Abraham.' The most solemn Abrahamic oath (sworn at Moriah after Isaac's near-sacrifice) is the covenant basis the Benedictus invokes as now being fulfilled.

76.note: You, child, will be called the prophet of the Most High, for you will go before the Lord to prepare his ways — the Malachi herald-promise applied to John by his father Zechariah

A voice of one calling: In the wilderness prepare the way for the LORD — the second forerunner text: the herald in the desert preparing the road for YHWH's return to Zion. Zechariah's blessing applies both the Malachi and Isaianic forerunner traditions to John, as Luke will later confirm explicitly (3:4-6).

78.note: The sunrise shall visit us from on high — the star arising from Jacob (Num 24:17); Zechariah's Benedictus identifies the Messiah's coming as the rising of the dawn-light

But for you who revere my name, the sun of righteousness will rise with healing in its rays — the 'rising sun' (anatole) that will come from heaven echoes Malachi's 'sun of righteousness rising with healing.' Both texts promise a dawn-figure bringing light and healing after a time of darkness and judgment. The Benedictus closes Zechariah's song by pointing to the coming dawn that John will announce.

79.note: The people walking in darkness have seen a great light; on those living in the land of deep darkness a light has dawned — Isaiah 9:2 is the direct source for 'shine on those living in darkness and in the shadow of death.' The Benedictus closes by pointing to the same Isaiah text that Matthew cited for the beginning of Jesus's Galilean ministry (Matt 4:16), establishing that the light Isaiah prophesied is the one John prepares.

Some sat in darkness and the deepest gloom, prisoners suffering in iron chains... He brought them out of darkness and the deepest gloom and broke away their chains — the psalm of redemption from darkness and shadow of death uses the same paired imagery (darkness / shadow of death) that Zechariah applies to those awaiting the dawn. The Benedictus draws on the psalmic tradition of YHWH delivering from death-darkness.

4.note: From Bethlehem a ruler — Jesus born there of Mary

11.note: A Savior, who is Christ the Lord — the child born to us who is wonderful counselor, mighty God, everlasting Father, prince of peace; the angelic announcement echoes the Isaianic throne-name proclamation
In the city of David — the Bethlehem origin of the Messiah from Mic 5:2; Bethlehem is now named as the site of the Savior's birth

14.note: Glory to God in the highest and on earth peace — YHWH blesses his people with peace (shalom); the angelic doxology enacts the Psalmic peace-blessing at the Messiah's birth

29.note: The Nunc Dimittis: mine eyes have seen your salvation — all the ends of the earth shall see the salvation of our God (Isa 52:10); Simeon sees the salvation in the infant and echoes the Isaianic universal-salvation vision

32.note: A light for revelation to the Gentiles and for glory to your people Israel — the Servant who is a light to the nations; Simeon's Nunc Dimittis applies the Servant vocation to the infant Jesus
Light for the Gentiles — Simeon: a light of revelation to the Gentiles

34.note: This child is appointed for the fall and rising of many in Israel — YHWH as a stone of offense and a rock of stumbling (Isa 8:14); Simeon echoes the divided response to the divine presence

4.note: The voice of one crying in the wilderness — Luke quotes Isa 40:3-5 more fully than Matthew/Mark, including the all flesh shall see the salvation of God (Isa 40:5); the universal scope is emphasized
Prepare the way of the Lord

22.note: You are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased — the baptism voice combining the royal Ps 2:7 with the Servant Isa 42:1; Luke's version is identical to Mark

18.note: The Spirit of the Lord is upon me because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor — Jesus reads Isa 61:1-2 in the Nazareth synagogue and declares it fulfilled in their hearing; the Spirit-anointed Servant's mission is his own mission manifesto
The Spirit of the Lord is upon me

25.note: Elijah was sent to a woman in Sidon — the widow of Zarephath as the OT precedent for grace to Gentiles over against unbelieving Israel; Jesus cites this when Nazareth rejects him

27.note: Naaman the Syrian was cleansed, none of the lepers in Israel were cleansed — the Gentile general over against the many Israelite lepers; the second precedent for Gentile grace cited at Nazareth
I have not come to call the righteous but sinners to repentance — the Hosea mercy-principle grounds Jesus's table-fellowship with Levi and the tax collectors

20.note: Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God — Luke's beatitudes are more direct (you rather than Matthew's they); the Isaianic poor (anawim) as the recipients of the Spirit's anointing

21.note: Blessed are you who are hungry now, for you shall be satisfied — the Isaianic eschatological banquet where the hungry are filled; the beatitude draws on the feast-theology of Isa 25

27.note: Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you — the Proverbs precedent for good toward enemies; Jesus radicalizes it from the neighbor to the enemy

35.note: Lend expecting nothing in return and your reward will be great — the righteous man who lends generously (Ps 37:26); the Psalmic generosity pattern extended by Luke's Jesus to enemy-lending

22.note: The blind receive their sight, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor have good news proclaimed — Luke's Isaianic sign-list (identical to Matthew) as Jesus's answer to John
Good news to the poor — the final item in the list identifying the Spirit-anointed ministry with the Servant's proclamation to the anawim

27.note: This is he of whom it is written: Behold I send my messenger before your face who will prepare your way — Jesus applies Mal 3:1 to John, declaring him the herald of the messianic visitation
I will send my messenger ahead of you

10.note: To you it has been given to know the secrets of the kingdom of God, but for others they are in parables so that seeing they may not see — Luke's abbreviated citation of Isa 6:9 for the parable-concealment

22.note: He commanded the storm and it became calm — Luke's storm-calming draws from the same Psalmic divine sovereignty over the sea; the disciples ask who this is that commands the wind and water

35.note: This is my Son, my Chosen One; listen to him — Luke's version adds my Chosen One (the Servant designation, Isa 42:1) alongside the Deut 18:15 listen-to-him command; the Transfiguration voice in Luke is the fullest Christological investiture

51.note: When the days drew near for him to be taken up, he set his face to go to Jerusalem — Elijah's being taken up (assumption) is the typological background for Luke's unique travel-narrative framing; Jesus journeys toward his own assumption-through-death

18.note: I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven — the fall of the day star (Isa 14:12) applied to Satan's defeat; the disciples' mission-success signals the eschatological overthrow of the enemy's dominion

27.note: You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your strength and with all your mind — the Shema; Luke's lawyer gives the correct answer before Jesus confirms it (unlike Matthew where Jesus gives the answer)

29.note: Sign of Jonah -- as Jonah was a sign to the Ninevites

31.note: The queen of the South came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon — the Sheba queen's recognition of Solomon as the reproach to this generation; something greater than Solomon is here

32.note: The men of Nineveh will rise up at the judgment and condemn this generation — Nineveh's repentance at Jonah's preaching as the witness against the unrepentant generation; something greater than Jonah is here
Nineveh repented at Jonah's preaching -- greater than Jonah is here

51.note: I came to bring division, not peace — a man's enemies are the men of his own house (Mic 7:6); the family-division resulting from loyalty to Christ echoes the Micah household-conflict passage

19.note: The mustard seed grew into a tree and the birds nested in its branches — the great tree of Daniel's vision (Dan 4:12); the kingdom's growth to become the shelter of all peoples echoes the Danielic universal-empire imagery

35.note: Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD — Luke's lament over Jerusalem ends with the Hosanna-Psalm; the cry will be raised at the triumphal entry (19:38), which becomes the last opportunity for Jerusalem's recognition

16.note: A man gave a great banquet and invited many — the Isaianic eschatological feast for all peoples; Luke's great banquet parable is the realization of this prophetic feast, with the excluded-and-invited reversal

On this mountain the LORD Almighty will prepare a feast of rich food for all peoples — the Great Banquet parable enacts Isaiah's eschatological feast on Zion. The host's invitation to 'all peoples' that the invited guests reject (vv.18-20) fulfills the Isaianic pattern: the feast prepared for Israel is opened to outsiders (vv.21-23) when Israel refuses.

4.note: The parable of the lost sheep: he leaves the 99 to seek the one — YHWH's promise to seek and find the lost, scattered sheep; Jesus acts as the divine shepherd enacting the Ezekielian restoration

I myself will search for my sheep and look after them. As a shepherd looks after his scattered flock when he is with them, so will I look after my sheep — the divine shepherd who searches for lost sheep (Ezek 34:11-12, 16) is the direct antecedent for the parable's searching shepherd. Jesus presents himself as YHWH fulfilling the Ezekiel promise to seek the lost personally.

20.note: The father saw him and was filled with compassion and ran — the compassion of the father for the returning son echoes YHWH's maternal compassion that will not forget the child; the parable grounds divine mercy in the parental-love tradition of Isaiah

13.note: But if serving the LORD seems undesirable to you, then choose for yourselves this day whom you will serve — Joshua's covenant-renewal challenge at Shechem (choose this day whom you will serve) is the structural antecedent for 'no one can serve two masters.' The Deuteronomic framework of undivided loyalty to YHWH (Deut 6:4-5) underlies the saying; Joshua's binary (serve YHWH or the gods of the land) is the model that Jesus applies to wealth.

How long will you waver between two opinions? If the LORD is God, follow him; but if Baal is God, follow him — Elijah's challenge at Carmel is the prophetic form of the two-masters principle: divided loyalty to YHWH and Baal is incoherent. Jesus's saying applies the same binary to God and mammon, treating wealth-service as the functional equivalent of Baal-worship in the new covenant context.

16.note: See, I will send the prophet Elijah before that great and dreadful day of the LORD comes — the Law and the Prophets proclaimed until John describes the era closed by Malachi's final word (the Elijah-promise that ends the OT canon). John's arrival is the fulfillment of Mal 4:5-6; the era of kingdom proclamation beginning with John is the era that Malachi's closing promise anticipated.

The Spirit of the Sovereign LORD is on me, because the LORD has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor — the 'good news of the kingdom of God being preached' since John (v.16) echoes the anointed herald of Isa 61:1-2 whom Jesus identified as himself (Luke 4:18-21). The preaching of the kingdom is the proclamation of the year of the LORD's favor.

17.note: Your word, LORD, is eternal; it stands firm in the heavens — the permanence of God's word in Ps 119:89 is the OT ground for Jesus's statement that it is easier for heaven and earth to pass than for one stroke of the Law to drop out. The Torah's permanence is grounded in the divine character; Jesus is not abrogating the Law but affirming its absolute divine authority, which his own fulfillment of it honors and confirms.

19.note: You lie on beds adorned with ivory and lounge on your couches. You dine on choice lambs and fattened calves... you drink wine by the bowlful and use the finest lotions, but you do not grieve over the ruin of Joseph — Amos's woe against the wealthy who feast while the poor suffer is the prophetic template for the rich man of Luke 16:19-31. The purple, fine linen, and daily luxury (v.19) describe precisely the Amos type; the neglect of Lazarus at his gate is the prophetic indictment enacted in narrative.

22.note: Then Abraham breathed his last and died at a good old age, an old man and full of years; and he was gathered to his people — the phrase 'gathered to his people' / resting with the ancestors is the OT idiom for the blessed death of the righteous patriarch. 'Abraham's bosom/side' (v.22) is the narrative development of this patriarchal rest: the righteous dead share the place of the covenant father, as Abraham himself was gathered to his people.

24.note: But you are our Father, though Abraham does not know us or Israel acknowledge us; you, LORD, are our Father, our Redeemer from of old is your name — Isaiah 63:16 notes that Abraham might not recognize his descendants in judgment, which makes the rich man's appeal 'Father Abraham' poignant and theologically ironic. The appeal to Abrahamic paternity (as also in John 8:39-44) does not guarantee participation in Abraham's blessing; faith and covenant faithfulness do.

29.note: So Moses wrote down this law and gave it to the Levitical priests... Read this law before all Israel in their hearing — 'Moses and the Prophets' as the authoritative witness invokes the canonical shape of the OT: the Torah given through Moses (Deut 31:9) and the prophetic writings that followed it. The sufficiency of these witnesses for guiding the living to repentance is the claim; the rich man's request for a resurrection sign is the request for additional evidence the Scripture has already provided.

31.note: But your dead will live, LORD; their bodies will rise — let those who dwell in the dust wake up and shout for joy — the prophetic promise of resurrection (Isa 26:19; Dan 12:2) is the OT backdrop for the parable's climax. 'Even if someone rises from the dead' anticipates Jesus's own resurrection and the Pharisees' rejection of it (Matt 28:11-15); the parable predicts that those who ignore Moses and the Prophets will not be convinced by the resurrection.

I am going to open your graves and bring you up from them; I will bring you back to the land of Israel — Ezekiel's valley of dry bones is the prophetic ground for resurrection hope. The parable's reference to 'someone rising from the dead' invokes this tradition. The rich man's implicit request for a resurrection-witness to persuade his brothers would have seemed to a Jewish audience like the fulfillment of Ezekiel's promise — but the parable predicts it will not suffice without prior covenant faithfulness.

26.note: As it was in the days of Noah — eating and drinking and marrying until the flood came; the sudden judgment pattern of the Noah-generation as the type of the parousia-judgment

28.note: As it was in the days of Lot — eating, drinking, buying, selling, planting, building until the destruction came from heaven; Sodom as the second type of sudden-judgment with life-as-usual beforehand

7.note: Will not God give justice to his elect who cry to him day and night? — the prophetic call to defend the oppressed and plead for justice (Isa 1:17); the persistent widow's cry echoes the persistent prayer of the righteous-afflicted that YHWH will vindicate

8.note: Zacchaeus's fourfold restitution — the Mosaic law requires fourfold restitution for a stolen ox (Exod 22:1); Zacchaeus applies the maximum restitution-standard to his fraudulent tax-collection, demonstrating genuine repentance

35.note: Your king comes on a donkey — Palm Sunday entry in Luke

38.note: Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the LORD — the triumphal entry Psalm; Luke adds the King identification that Matthew and Mark leave implied; the entry is explicitly the fulfillment of Ps 118's messianic processional

46.note: My house shall be a house of prayer — Luke's temple cleansing citation omits for all nations (included in Mark); the temple's prayer-purpose is the standard violated
My house shall be called a house of prayer

17.note: The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone — Luke's citation of Ps 118:22 as the rejected-and-vindicated stone applied to Jesus's rejection by the leaders and his resurrection-vindication

37.note: Moses showed that the dead are raised when he called the Lord the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob — Luke's resurrection argument from the burning bush, identical to Matthew and Mark
Even Moses showed at the burning bush -- God of the living

42.note: The LORD said to my Lord: Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies your footstool — Luke's David's-son question using Ps 110:1 to demonstrate the Messiah's transcendence of Davidic lineage

25.note: Signs in the sun and moon and stars — the Isaianic cosmic-sign language of the Day of the LORD; Luke's Olivet Discourse uses the same prophetic sign-framework

27.note: The Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory — Luke's Danielic coming-in-cloud citation for the eschatological parousia

20.note: This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood — Luke (and Paul in 1 Cor 11:25) preserves the explicit new covenant language from Jer 31:31; the cup is identified as the new covenant enactment
The covenant ratified by blood — Moses threw the covenant blood on the people; the Passover cup becomes the new covenant blood that ratifies the new Moses-moment

37.note: This scripture must be fulfilled in me: And he was numbered with the transgressors — Luke's Jesus quotes Isa 53:12 explicitly as being fulfilled in his coming arrest and crucifixion; the clearest direct Isaianic Servant citation by Jesus of himself

30.note: They will say to the mountains, Fall on us, and to the hills, Cover us — Hosea's oracle of judgment when Israelites will want to hide from divine wrath; Jesus quotes it to the Jerusalem women as the judgment coming on the city

34.note: They cast lots to divide his garments — Luke's passion narrative draws from Ps 22 as Matthew and Mark do; the garment-division detail from Ps 22:18

36.note: They offered him sour wine — Ps 69:21 (they gave me vinegar to drink); the mockery at the cross draws from the Psalms of righteous suffering

46.note: Father, into your hands I commit my spirit — Jesus dies with Ps 31:5 on his lips (the evening prayer of Jewish piety: into your hand I commit my spirit); the righteous sufferer of Ps 31 trusting YHWH to the end
Into your hands I commit my spirit

19.note: Jesus of Nazareth, a man who was a prophet mighty in deed and word — the Emmaus disciples describe Jesus in terms of the Mosaic prophet-category; the disciples have not yet understood he is more than a prophet

27.note: Beginning with Moses and all the Prophets he interpreted to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself — the risen Christ reads all of Scripture as pointing to himself; from Gen 3:15 (the first promise) through every subsequent covenant and prophecy, the entire canonical story finds its fulfillment in the crucified and risen Christ

44.note: Everything written about me in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms must be fulfilled — Luke's risen-Christ hermeneutic: the three-part Hebrew canon (Torah, Nevi'im, Ketuvim) all pointed to him; his resurrection is the key that unlocks what was always there
